speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] is like, what are you doing? [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. But he's gonna to see this. So you can say, 'hi' to him. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who said that? [INTERPOSING VOICES] He wanted to know which one was Steven Spielberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] Say, bravo, bravo. Come on. Bravo! See, he's clapping. I taught him this.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number six of the interview with Yvette Lennon, on February 12, 1995. OK, would you like to introduce everyone here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is my husband, Jim. This is my daughter, Peggy; her husband, Peter. This is Sean. This is Michael. And this is Paul.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sean was the one who wanted to know who was Steven Spielberg?

speaker: interviewer
text: So have you spoken with-- with them about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. They know everything. We talk all the time about it. It's matter of fact-- just here and there, things that happen-- you know, reminding. And my brother also was here. You know, when he's here, he talks sometimes-- or when we are there. Yeah. We just-- because it always comes back. Like, we talk about it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- so do you tell 'em like the whole story-- everything? Or just some snippets here and there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know if I told you. Did I ever tell you the whole story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I mean, all in once. Because, you know, we-- sometimes you remember things that-- and you repeat it. You-- you tell them again, you know. But, yeah, they know the whole story. They do.

speaker: interviewer
text: We always talk We talk. We-- even when my-- with my sister, when-- we would talk with her. We talk with my brother. And it will always comes to this subject a lot-- when we talk. So-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what else do you have to tell? [BACKROUND NOISE] Well-- [LAUGHTER] do you appreciate the-- do they appreciate the importance of what you went through?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do they-- they-- would they like to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I'd just-- in my life, what it's done, is heightened-- PAUL (GRANDSON): [MOANING] --you know, a concern for-- for all aspects of human life, you know, things like euthanasia in our country. 'Cause now there's a big push for euthanasia. And again, [? Gershenowitz ?] and all this-- and it's going to be a--

speaker: interviewee
text: You have something for the hand?

speaker: interviewee
text: --concern for the humanity of the individual, and how that has-- it's really sacred, you know-- human life.

speaker: interviewee
text: You wanted to say something, Michael? MICHAEL (GRANDSON): We-- we saw a tape on it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't we have something to give him? MICHAEL (GRANDSON): Not like-- PAUL (GRANDSON): [MOANING] [INTERPOSING VOICES] MICHAEL (GRANDSON): --but we saw, like, a Holocaust tape-- this other movie. PAUL (GRANDSON): [CRYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: What, what, what, what what, what, what? PAUL (GRANDSON): [MOANING] What? What? What? MICHAEL (GRANDSON): We know. PAUL (GRANDSON): [CRYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: I think Paul wants to say something. [LAUGHS] PAUL (GRANDSON): [CRYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: He doesn't know you, that's it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who's that? Look at all those people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Look at that! Look at that. PAUL (GRANDSON): [MOANING]

speaker: interviewee
text: Here. PAUL (GRANDSON): [MOANING] Ah, de, de, da, da. Look, look.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now look-- look at the camera. PAUL (GRANDSON): [MOANING]

speaker: interviewee
text: Look at that. Look at that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Look at that. Look at that. PAUL (GRANDSON): [MOANING] I guess he's not going have a film career. PAUL (GRANDSON): [MOANING] [LAUGHTER] SEAN (GRANDSON): Come on. PAUL (GRANDSON): [CRYING] Ah-uh, ah-uh. PAUL (GRANDSON): [CRYING] Whoa! How did we know that we shouldn't bring you?

speaker: interviewee
text: How did we know? Look, Pauly. Look.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, well, granddad is gonna give you something-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: What is that? Oh, he has a jar. Here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, something for you to throw. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Make him sit. Make him sit. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah, see, now he's got something to play with.

speaker: interviewee
text: He's very interested in this jar.

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, from when we were very little, my mother used to speak to us about it. Like, we'd just be, you know, having a bath, even, or something like that. She'd be speaking from really young, I mean, 5-years-old-- like, you know, from that age on. And it was always like second nature hearing about this information.

speaker: interviewer
text: It didn't hit me, you know, what an atrocity it was until we were much bigger. We really took it for granted. And when I was in high school, people would say, your mother, you know, went through that? And I was like, well, again, it was just an automatic thing. We were used to hearing about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And now she plans on-- she's been doing all this-- I'm sure she said this today-- all these interviews in the schools with the children, and these different talks. And she'll be doing it for these two guys in their school. And this age group, they were concerned-- he's only 6, 7-- that age range. And they said, well, it's just a little early for them to be hearing about this stuff. But I remember hearing about it all the way from when we were younger than him.

speaker: interviewer
text: PAUL (GRANDSON): [MOANING] And you don't think it was too-- too much [BACKROUND NOISE] for you to hear?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I think she-- she edited out, of course, all the things that were too difficult to hear maybe at that age. But, I just-- on the one hand, you know, we did have nightmares growing up and things like that. You know, you always-- PAUL (GRANDSON): [MOANING] You know, you always think that maybe they could-- they could come and take you. It kind of makes you a little insecure about things. But I would rather have known. I was glad she told us from the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, Mrs. Lennon, do you think this-- it affected the way you brought up your children, the way you raised your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: The way-- well, I-- I-- I-- I did talk to them about the Holocaust, but the most of the time, sometimes we used to laugh at the things we did in the camp, you know, and then with them, with the kids, I mean. You remember, Peggy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we saw-- I mean, they didn't treat me like a mother, that we can't talk to about the Holocaust, 'cause we can't be-- do these things to her, because she went through the Holocaust. They all-- they gave me a hard time, too, like other mothers. It's not-- you know, like how do they do to the mothers, no. Because usually, I find out that many mothers are-- they don't want to talk about this. PAUL (GRANDSON): [MOANING] And the children-- and the children, they don't know what to do. And they have to see a psychiatrist and that, because they get-- because they see their mothers doesn't-- don't say anything. But something is there that's wrong. So they have to talk-- to tell them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So do you think it helps you to talk about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, I-- I always did talk about this, because, like I said, I'm not gonna let this-- the-- the Nazis to-- to rule the rest of my life, to ruin it for me. Because if I start saying-- being upset about it and everything, then, what good is it that you stayed alive? Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that in some ways it's made you strong, what you went through? PAUL (GRANDSON): [MOANING]

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, well this-- this tape is gonna be passed down, so maybe you-- do you all want to say, 'hi'?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Paul, why you scream? Now you not talking.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, maybe he had the last word.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah it's-- [INTERPOSING VOICES] MICHAEL (GRANDSON): And he gave up the jar. --last thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this picture is, from left to right, this is my sister Lily. Then it's me. I was expecting Peggy; and my mother-in-law, who came to stay with us for nine months. And-- and Peggy's at the bottom. Actually, I was expecting David. [LAUGHS] I can see now, the picture. Yeah. This is my friend Olga, who is now in Israel. And she was in the camp with me. Olga Bendrueben is her name-- was her name. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is me in England, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: Shepherd's Bush, that's where we were. Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I saw it before when you say, yeah, it's OK. Because I saw--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us what it is?

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what year was it taken? What?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know when he took this pictures. I don't know if it's written on the back.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my sister with her school friends. Shall I say the name--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure. Do you want to go from left to right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, from left to right-- Rebecca; the other one is Yvonne; Rachele, my sister, Lily, another Rebecca, and I can't remember her name. She was-- you're not taping me, are you, while I'm talking?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, but it's OK. It's just for reference.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's fine. Do you know roughly--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she was one of her friends. Well, back when she was in school-- I don't know what grade she was in-- what she was--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was-- this was before the Nazis--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, this is the group. That was the day we came to Athens, when we came back from the concentration camp. But I don't remember all the names. The two first ones on the-- on top, on the-- on the left, are sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then comes Julie. Julie's that you one that I kept-- I kept saying that she was living with us, and she has her finger-- something wrong with her finger. Minache-- she-- she married the doctor on-- Dr. Minache, who is the director and the doctor of the old age home here in town. You remember him? He died the age of 94, last year. And he wrote a book on his life-- life as Dr. Minache.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then there's another girl, which I don't remember. Then it's me, Yvette. And there's Lily, the last one. Now, the second row is-- I don't know his name-- neither the next one. The middle one, Salvatore Valsano is his name. That's the group that we made friends with when we came back. And-- then, I don't know the other one. But then next to him, one before the last is [NON-ENGLISH] is his name. He also was part of our group in Salonica. Now the last-- the bottom line, is Michele on the left. And I don't remember the name of the other one.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my brother, Michele, and my cousin, Frieda. And she's the one who went willingly to the gas chambers.

speaker: interviewee
text: She had her sister, Nita. She-- they had chosen her too, but she wasn't chosen.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, can you tell us about this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is my grandmother and me, when I was four years old. [PAUSES FOR 17 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: It's when my daughter got married. He played at her wedding. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, you-- you'll tell us that on tape.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, can you tell us about this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is the day of my daughter's wedding. And somebody sang the song that she wanted. And he played the violin-- the viola.

speaker: interviewee
text: David, my son, David. Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is also my son, David. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] He doesn't have a beard anymore. [PAUSES FOR 16 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, can you tell us a little bit about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, this book was written by one of the girls, the French girl. The-- Fania Fenelon, about this-- she wrote this book. But we didn't know. She didn't even know we were alive. This is how she writes it. At the end of the book she says, 'I don't know what happened to the Greeks, Yvette and Lily.' She says, because she tried to find-- she tried to find everybody. She found many of them, like you see. But then my husband-- then she came to New York to promote her book. And my husband was-- was at the job. And he saw in the newspapers what Fania Fenelon was here in New York promoting her book. So he called up the place and he says, if Ms. Fenelon wants to know what happened to the Greeks, one of them is my wife. And she says, give me their phone numbers, she went. So we spend the whole day with my sisters. And I have it all in tape. I taped it. We were talking French, Spanish, Greek, because she's-- she's French. And-- so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now this was just two weeks ago. Could you tell us a bit about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: When did you write the paper, Jim?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this picture-- well, this paper is-- that I had-- I was, how do you call? I-- not videotaped, I was like you, what you did.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was interviewed by this girl. And she happens to be a true German. She's not--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just hold up your hand there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is written on there, the name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Berger. And she's not-- she's German, a real German. So she-- she wanted to interview me, and she came here. And we talked for a while.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then she-- she put this in the paper. We didn't know that she was going put it in the paper. And that's all, what this picture, what it's all about. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: See, this is Fania Fenelon there. And sees my sister's house when she came. And this is the-- the dog when she's saying good-bye.

speaker: interviewee
text: My song to [? pieces ?] till the street. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want us to put this on film?

speaker: interviewee
text: If you-- what, this? So. This is Fania Fenelon to see us once she found out we are here. Oh, there's one--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so-- so who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: What is this? On the left is my sister. In the middle is Fania Fenelon. And I am on the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And so that book-- that there was-- we saw that book. And we saw that chart that's mimeographed from the French version of the book, that shows the placement of everyone--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's my cousin here from France.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she wrote the book. See, in France, they came out first, the book. And there-- there's an excellent chapter--

speaker: interviewee
text: Besides-- and they had-- you see the-- all this-- like, knowing who we are. And like, in this book, they don't say who we are in the English book.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's this-- this picture now that we're looking at?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fania Fenelon. My sister is first at the door. And she's saying goodbye to Fania Fenelon, the one with the black coat.
